1: Growing pigs' diets with increased phytase activity and reduced available phosphorus resulted in similar performance and environmental impacts
G.A. Araujo a,* J.S. Martins V.T. Santos A.N.T.R. Monteiro P.C. Pozza a
a Department of Animal Science, State University of Maringá, 87023-105 Maringá, Brazil b Animine, 74000 Annecy, France

R T I C L INFO|ABSTRACT Article history: Received 9 August 2023 Revised 1 November 2023 Accepted 3 November 2023 Available online 30 November 2023 Handling editor: Marjeta Candek-Potokar Keywords: Life cycle Nitrogen Performance Phytic acid Swine|The objective of this study was to evaluate, through a life cycle assessment, the environmental impacts of the postweaning growth of pigs fed with diets with reduced levels of available phosphorus and increased phytases. In Trial I (nitrogen and phosphorus balance), 25 crossbreed barrow pigs (BP 400 X BM 500 Biriba's), with an average weight of 23.05 + 3.24 kg and average starting age of 60.75 + 4.73 days, were assigned to five dietary treatments using a randomized block design, with five replications and each ani- mal as an experimental unit. Five experimental diets were evaluated: each increasing phytase activity units (FTU) (control [0], 250, 500, 750 and 1 000 FTU) and decreasing levels of available phosphorus (0.435, 0.375, 0.315, 0.255 and 0.195%), respectively. Due to phytase supplementation, all diets meet the requirements for available phosphorus. The pigs received two daily meals. Total collection of feces was performed, adding 2 g of ferric oxide as a fecal marker. Feces were collected daily, stored in plastic bags and frozen. All excreted urine was collected in plastic buckets containing 20 mL of HCI (1:1), and a 20% aliquot was frozen for further analysis. In Trial II (performance), 40 crossbreed barrow pigs (BP 400 X BM 500 Biriba's) with an initial average weight of 15.11 + 0.67 kg, average starting age of 50.63 + 0.81 and finishing age of 72.43 + 2.07 days were assigned using a randomized block design to five dietary treatments, with eight replications and each animal as an experimental unit. The pigs were weighed at the beginning and at the end of the trial, as were the feed supplies and refusals. These data were used to calculate the feed conversion ratio, average daily gain and average daily feed intake. The life cycle analysis was based on the CML 2001 (baseline) method V3.02, as implemented in the Simapro soft- ware, version 8.05 (PRé Consultant, 2014). These data may further serve as a reference for the study of the impact of phytase supplementation during other breeding phases, or even in other animals whose envi- ronmental impacts are being studied. © 2023 The Author(s). 3: There is worldwide concern regarding the environmental impact of livestock production. Consequently, there is much

research currently looking for alternatives to mitigate environmen- tal impacts using nutrition as a tool. Phosphorus is an important mineral in animal nutrition. However, it also has the potential to become an environmental polluter, thus birthing the idea of this work to reduce the available phosphorus content while still meet- ing nutritional requirements, given the ability of phytases to release phosphorous from phytic acid. We observed that adding phytases, while reducing the available phosphorous content, could not reduce environmental impacts, but presented the same growth performance.

2: Global consumer demand for protein has increased in the past years, with the search for high-quality meat also demanding greater awareness of climate change from the livestock industry, like in pig production, and the delivery of products with reduced environmen- tal impacts. Currently, there is research looking for ways to mitigate these impacts via nutrition, because feed provides about half of the available impact in pig production (Gerber et al., 2013). There is worldwide concern regarding the environmental impact of livestock, thus limiting the available land designated for animal production, leading the way to intensified productivity methods (Saath and Fachinello, 2018). Most cereal grains and oilseeds have a significant percentage of phosphorus bonded in phytic acid format, also known as myo-inositol hexaphosphate. This chemical compound has the function of storing phosphorus in seeds. However, it is practically unavailable to non-ruminant animals (Raboy, 2020). Phosphorus is an important mineral in animal nutrition, with high concentrations in hard tissues, but lower in soft tissues (Cromwell, 2005).

Dicalcium phosphate is supplemented in diets. However, one future problem for modern agriculture could be that it is depen- dent on phosphorus, which is not renewable and could run out in about 100 years (Cordell et al., 2009). Additionally, pig manure has high phosphorus concentrations, so it can potentially stimulate the growth of seaweeds, increasing the competition for oxygen among these protists and other aquatic animals if mishandled (Moreira et al., 2010). An alternative to reduce the excretion of phosphorus could be the use of exogenous enzymes like phytases, in order to degrade the phytic acid (Leytem et al., 2004). Phytases could be added to the animal feed, reducing the need for dietary dicalcium phosphate to achieve the animal's requirements (Lei et al., 2013) in order to keep the same growth performance in pigs associated with the reduced phosphorus excretion.

Therefore, the pig supply chain is a complex system, requiring the production of fertilizers, pesticides, land transformation, water usage crops and animals, energy and fossil fuel, as well as other parts of the chain (McAuliffe et al., 2016). In this way, the life cycle assessment (LCA) evaluates the environmental impact as a whole, and has been used in recent years in swine production (McAuliffe et al., 2016). Nevertheless, most LCA studies use average data input, which cre- ates uncertainty in the results due to the diversity of production sys- items, farming practices, location, climate and genetics (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005). The influence of the animal-to-animal vari- ability in pig performance and requirements has been demonstrated in different studies (Brossard et al., 2009; Brossard et al., 2014) and could affect performance, excretion and the LCA results.

Therefore, the aim of this study was to evaluate the environ- mental impact of growing pigs fed with different levels of phytase and reduced available phosphorus, based on the LCA methodology, with individually observed data on performance and excretion.

5: The definition of system and subsystem boundaries was derived from Nguyen et al. (2010). The LCA considered the activity of piglet production from 15 to 30 kg BW, in five different scenarios of feed formulation, including crop production, grain drying and process- ing, feed production and transport, growing pig production, and manure storage, transport and spreading. The impacts were calcu- lated at the farm gate, and the functional unit considered was one kg of BW gain over the growing period. The growing pig production system considered was a conventional nursery farm with indoor rearing of castrated males on partially slated floor, and the collec- tion and storage of liquid manure as slurry.
6: Resource use and emissions associated with the production and delivery of supplies for crop production (fertilizers, pesti- cides, tractor fuel and agricultural machinery) came from the Ecoinvent database version 3 (SimaPro LCA software 8.0, PRé Consultants). Energy usage in the building for lights and ventila- tion was considered, but not the emissions and resources used for the construction of the facilities, nor the land occupied by the buildings. Veterinary drugs and hygiene products were also left out, as proposed by Garcia-Launay et al. (2014). We presumed that soybean was produced in Southern Brazil, the second largest producer in the country (CONAB, 2016). Life cycle inventory for maize and soybean were obtained from Alvarenga et al. (2012), and da Silva et al. (2010), respectively. For the grain-drying, and storage stage, data from Marques (2006) were utilized. For soybean meal and soybean oil, the resource and emissions were economically allocated, following the suggestion of Ramirez (2009).

Data for salt, phosphate, sodium bicarbonate, premix and lime- stone production used in the diet came from Wilfart et al. (2016). The antioxidant and growth promoter were assumed to have the same impacts as the premix. L-Lysine HCI, DL-methionine and L- threonine inventories were obtained from Mosnier et al. (2011). Production of L-tryptophan, L-valine and L-isoleucine was pre- sumed requires twice the amount of resources and energy produc- tion as those of L-lysine HCI (Garcia-Launay et al., 2014). The phytase data came from the Agribalyse database (2022). The pig production system was located in Southern Brazil, as described by Cherubini et al. (2015). The grains used in diets were assumed to be produced in the main producing areas of Southern Brazil. For the transportation inventory, the methodology reported by da Silva et al. (2010) was used. The imported products were pre- sumed to be mainly transported by sea, followed by road (Mosnier et al., 2011).

Five experimental diets were evaluated: increasing phytase activity units (FTU) (control (0), 250, 500, 750 and 1 000 FTU) and decreasing levels of available phosphorus (0.435, 0.375, 0.315, 0.255 and 0.195%). The diets were formulated to meet the requirements proposed by Rostagno et al. (2017) (Table 1). Diets were formulated to have the same metabolizable energy content. Sodium bicarbonate was used to keep the electrolyte balance equalized across the experimental diets. The feed production pro- cess at the feed factory was included in the inventory, considering that it would be held in the same region as the pig production (Garcia-Launay et al., 2014). All procedures were performed in accordance with Brazilian guidelines reviewed and approved by the Ethics Committee of the State University Maringá (CEUA proto- col No. 2032070222).

9: Twenty-five crossbreed barrow piglets (BP 400 BM 500 Biri- ba's with an average weight of 23.05 + 3.24 kg and average start- ing age of 60.75 + 4.73 days were housed in metabolic cages in a partially controlled environment using air conditioning equip- ment. The experimental design was set in randomized blocks, replicated in time, with five treatments, as previously described, and five replicates. The experimental unit consisted of a pig. The pigs received two daily meals. The total daily amount of feed was determined according to the intake in the adaptation phase that was recorded during the period of seven days, based on meta- bolic weight (BW0.75). To avoid waste and facilitate handling, water was added to the diets (1 mL/3 g of feed) and, after each meal, water was supplied at the rate of 3 mL/1 g of feed, calculated for each experimental unit in order to avoid excessive water intake. To calculate the nitrogen and phosphorus balance, total fecal col- lection was performed, adding 2 g ferric oxide (Fe2O3) as a fecal marker. Feces were collected daily, stored in plastic bags, and kept in a freezer (-18 °C). Urine was fully collected in plastic buckets containing 20 mL of HCI 1:1, and a 20% sample was collected daily and frozen. Both feces and urine were collected for five days.
10: Forty crossbred barrows (BP 400 X BM 500) with an initial aver- age weight of 15.1 + 0.67 kg and final weight of 31.9 + 2.62 were used, average starting age of 50.63 + 0.81, and end of the trial age of 72.43 + 2.07 days. The animals were allotted in a random- ized blocks design with five treatments (the same used in Experi- ment 1), eight replicates and one animal per experimental unit. The pigs were weighed at the beginning and at the end of the trial, as were the feed supplies and refusals. These data were used to cal- culate feed conversion (FC), average daily gain (ADG) and average daily feed intake (ADFI).

Representative feed samples were analyzed according to the Association of Official Analytical Chemists (AOAC, 2006) for DM (method 950.05), ash (method 942.05), crude fiber (method 962.09) and total nitrogen (method 984.13). The phosphorus con- tent of the feed and feces samples were obtained using UV-Vis spectrophotometry (model Cary 50 Varian Inc.). The phosphorus content of the urine was quantified by the molybdate phosphorus method Goldanalisa®, using a spectrophotometer BIO-2000 (Bio- plus Ltda., Barueri, Brazil) at wavelengths of 650 nm. Urine and feces samples were also analyzed for total nitrogen, DM and ash. The environmental consequences of manure utilization were eval- uated using system expansion, as described by Nguyen et al. (2010). Thus, manure produced was assumed to substitute a cer- tain amount of mineral fertilizers by using a mineral fertilizer equivalency (MFE, %). We assumed that the MFE was 75% of the total nitrogen in manure (Nguyen et al., 2010), with 5% extra loss as nitrates compared to mineral fertilizers (Garcia-Launay et al., 2014), and MFE was 100% for phosphorus (Sommer et al., 2008).

Emissions were estimated for NH3, N20, NO and CH4, as described by Monteiro et al. (2016). The building's NH3 emissions during manure storage were calculated according to emission fac- tors proposed by Rigolot et al. (2010), considering the effects of ambient temperature. The amount of nitrogen, phosphorus and organic matter excreted by each pig were obtained from laboratory analysis, associated with data from experiment I.

We based our analysis on the CML 2 001 (baseline) method V3.02, as implemented in the Simapro software, version 8.05 (PRé Consultants, 2014), and added the following categories: land occupation from CML 2001 (all categories) version 2.04, and total cumulative energy demand version 1.8 (non-renewable fossil + nu- clear). Thus, we considered pig production's global warming poten- tial (GWP) (kg CO2-eq.), eutrophication potential (EP) (g PO4-eq.), acidification potential (AP) (g SO2-eq.), terrestrial ecotoxicity (TE)

(g 1,4-DCB-eq.), cumulative energy demand (CED)(MJ), and land occupation (LO) (m - year). The GWP was calculated according to the 100-year global warming potential factors in kg CO2-eq.

The nitrogen and phosphorus excretion were calculated based on the nutrient intake of each pig (Tables 2 and 3) according to data determined in Trial I. The LCA calculations were performed for each pig according to its individual performance and excretion from 15 to 30 kg BW, on average. These calculations were per- formed using a calculation model developed using the SAS soft- ware (Statistical Analysis System, version 9.2).

Performance responses and environmental impacts were sub- jected to analysis of variance using the PROC GLM procedure (Sta- tistical Analysis System, version 9.2). The statistical model included the effects of diet level and block. In the case of significant effects of the diet on the parameter, regression analysis was per- formed. The degrees of freedom regarding phytase levels were par- titioned into orthogonal polynomials by using the mixed procedure of SAS (Statistical Analysis System, version 9.2) to fit the regression equations (linear and quadratic). A linear response plateau model was fitted using the SAEG-UFV software (2007) when the quadratic model was significative. For regressions, phytase FTU is considered the X value. Differences between treatments were considered significant at P < 0.05. All statistical analyses were performed using SAS.

We assumed that the MFE was 75% of the total nitrogen in manure (Nguyen et al., 2010), with 5% extra loss as nitrates compared to mineral fertilizers (Garcia-Launay et al., 2014), and MFE was 100% for phosphorus (Sommer et al., 2008).

Emissions were estimated for NH3, N20, NO and CH4, as described by Monteiro et al. (2016). The building's NH3 emissions during manure storage were calculated according to emission fac- tors proposed by Rigolot et al. (2010), considering the effects of ambient temperature. The amount of nitrogen, phosphorus and organic matter excreted by each pig were obtained from laboratory analysis, associated with data from experiment I.

We based our analysis on the CML 2 001 (baseline) method V3.02, as implemented in the Simapro software, version 8.05 (PRé Consultants, 2014), and added the following categories: land occupation from CML 2001 (all categories) version 2.04, and total cumulative energy demand version 1.8 (non-renewable fossil + nu- clear). Performance responses and environmental impacts were sub- jected to analysis of variance using the PROC GLM procedure (Sta- tistical Analysis System, version 9.2). The phosphorus con- tent of the feed and feces samples were obtained using UV-Vis spectrophotometry (model Cary 50 Varian Inc.). The phosphorus content of the urine was quantified by the molybdate phosphorus method Goldanalisa®, using a spectrophotometer BIO-2000 (Bio- plus Ltda., Barueri, Brazil) at wavelengths of 650 nm. Urine and feces samples were also analyzed for total nitrogen, DM and ash. The environmental consequences of manure utilization were eval- uated using system expansion, as described by Nguyen et al. (2010). Thus, manure produced was assumed to substitute a cer- tain amount of mineral fertilizers by using a mineral fertilizer equivalency (MFE, %). We assumed that the MFE was 75% of the total nitrogen in manure (Nguyen et al., 2010), with 5% extra loss as nitrates compared to mineral fertilizers (Garcia-Launay et al., 2014), and MFE was 100% for phosphorus (Sommer et al., 2008).

Emissions were estimated for NH3, N20, NO and CH4, as described by Monteiro et al. (2016). The building's NH3 emissions during manure storage were calculated according to emission fac- tors proposed by Rigolot et al. (2010), considering the effects of ambient temperature. The amount of nitrogen, phosphorus and organic matter excreted by each pig were obtained from laboratory analysis, associated with data from experiment I.

We based our analysis on the CML 2 001 (baseline) method V3.02, as implemented in the Simapro software, version 8.05 (PRé Consultants, 2014), and added the following categories: land occupation from CML 2001 (all categories) version 2.04, and total cumulative energy demand version 1.8 (non-renewable fossil + nu- clear). Thus, we considered pig production's global warming poten- tial (GWP) (kg CO2-eq.), eutrophication potential (EP) (g PO4-eq.), acidification potential (AP) (g SO2-eq.), terrestrial ecotoxicity (TE)

(g 1,4-DCB-eq.), cumulative energy demand (CED)(MJ), and land occupation (LO) (m - year). The GWP was calculated according to the 100-year global warming potential factors in kg CO2-eq.

The nitrogen and phosphorus excretion were calculated based on the nutrient intake of each pig (Tables 2 and 3) according to data determined in Trial I. The LCA calculations were performed for each pig according to its individual performance and excretion from 15 to 30 kg BW, on average. These calculations were per- formed using a calculation model developed using the SAS soft- ware (Statistical Analysis System, version 9.2).

Performance responses and environmental impacts were sub- jected to analysis of variance using the PROC GLM procedure (Sta- tistical Analysis System, version 9.2). The statistical model included the effects of diet level and block. In the case of significant effects of the diet on the parameter, regression analysis was per- formed. The degrees of freedom regarding phytase levels were par- titioned into orthogonal polynomials by using the mixed procedure of SAS (Statistical Analysis System, version 9.2) to fit the regression equations (linear and quadratic). A linear response plateau model was fitted using the SAEG-UFV software (2007) when the quadratic model was significative. For regressions, phytase FTU is considered the X value. Differences between treatments were considered significant at P < 0.05. All statistical analyses were performed using SAS.

also reduced the N in urine and total N excretion. Phytases may provide improved ileal digestibility of amino acids (Kemme et al., 1999), because the phosphorylated phytic acid and its esters (penta-, tetra-, tri-, di- and mono-inositol esters) have amino acid binding capacity (Yu et al., 2012). According to Yu et al. (2012), phytic acid may be hydrolyzed by fungal and E. coli phytases, and the binding capacity was tested in soy protein molecules, including the penta-ester isomers.

The obtained results showed that phytic acid has a strong bind- ing capacity to corn and soybean meal amino acids, with reduced bond strength when the molecule lost its phosphate radicals. The generally accepted concept is that the negatively charged phytate molecules form a binary protein-phytate complex with proteins, carrying a net positive charge at a pH lower than their isoelectric point. At a pH exceeding their isoelectric point, with proteins car- rying a net negative charge, a cationic bridge, usually with Ca2+ links the phytate and protein in salt-like complexes (Selle et al., 2012). However, the exact factors that determine how phytases could actually improve the ileal digestibility of amino acids remains unclear (Selle et al., 2012; Zouaoui et al., 2012). In this sense, the lower N excreted at higher phytase levels studied may be associated with an improved ileal digestibility of amino acids.

The 6-phytase metabolism pathway acts by reducing the phytic acid (inositol hexaphosphate or IP6), which is composed of an inos- itol ring bonded with six phosphate molecules. Therefore, the 6- phytase has the capacity to reduce to an inositol monophosphate molecule and releasing five molecules of inorganic phosphate that could be absorbed by the animals (Kryukov et al., 2021). According to Sands et al. (2001) and Oryschak et al. (2002), every parameter of phosphorus balance was affected by phytase, which is similar to the results obtained in this study (Table 3).

The fecal and excreted phosphorus were decreased according to the increased phytase addition (Table 2), as reported by Almeida and Stein (2012), due to the higher phosphorus digestion. These results show that exogenous phytases can reduce phytate due to its in vivo enzymatic activity since, the more phytase is added into the feed, the less available/total phosphorus is required in the diet, and less phosphorus will be excreted to the environment. In addi-

tion, we found that retained phosphorus linearly decreased with the addition of phytases, but this could be explained mainly with the lower phosphorus intake affecting retention. In many plant- based ingredients, phytic acid is a common compound acting as a way to store phosphorus. Pigs have limited endogenous phytases, having worse phosphorous retention, and excreting more phos- phorus due to their loss of phytic acid (Romano and Kumar, 2018).

The hypothesis of this study was that the addition of phytases, concomitant with the reduction of the available phosphorus con- tent in the form of dicalcium phosphate, could result in a similar environmental impact from pig production while still meeting the minimum available phosphorus requirements. Phytase supple- mentation resulted in the same impacts compared with diets con- taining high available phosphorus content without phytase supplementation.

The final values are expressed per kg of live weight (Table 6). Thus, the environmental impacts are highly dependent on animal performance and feed intake, and performance is linked to feed production and manure (Fig. 3). Efficient pig production could result in a lower ADF and, therefore, a lesser need for feed produc- tion. Manure also has an important share in the environmental impacts (Fig. 3), meaning that efficient production could reduce the 15-30 kg finishing phase, which could lead to fewer days excreting feces and urine.

The environmental impacts of all LCA categories were reduced per kg of diet (Table 5), according to phytase supplementation and dietary dicalcium phosphate reduction. Despite this study not finding any difference in environmental impacts when calcu- lating pig production as a whole, the strategy of using dietary phy- tases could help reduce the environmental impacts of the diets.

However, no effects on GWP, AP, EP, CED and LO were observed while analyzing the production of pigs (Table 6), mainly due to the same performance observed (Table 4). These results are similar to those found by Kebreab et al. (2016), observing that diets with phytases showed the same GWP as other diets, in a scenario with-

Phytases may provide improved ileal digestibility of amino acids (Kemme et al., 1999), because the phosphorylated phytic acid and its esters (penta-, tetra-, tri-, di- and mono-inositol esters) have amino acid binding capacity (Yu et al., 2012). According to Yu et al. (2012), phytic acid may be hydrolyzed by fungal and E. coli phytases, and the binding capacity was tested in soy protein molecules, including the penta-ester isomers.

The obtained results showed that phytic acid has a strong bind- ing capacity to corn and soybean meal amino acids, with reduced bond strength when the molecule lost its phosphate radicals. The generally accepted concept is that the negatively charged phytate molecules form a binary protein-phytate complex with proteins, carrying a net positive charge at a pH lower than their isoelectric point. At a pH exceeding their isoelectric point, with proteins car- rying a net negative charge, a cationic bridge, usually with Ca2+ links the phytate and protein in salt-like complexes (Selle et al., 2012). However, the exact factors that determine how phytases could actually improve the ileal digestibility of amino acids remains unclear (Selle et al., 2012; Zouaoui et al., 2012). In this sense, the lower N excreted at higher phytase levels studied may be associated with an improved ileal digestibility of amino acids.

The 6-phytase metabolism pathway acts by reducing the phytic acid (inositol hexaphosphate or IP6), which is composed of an inos- itol ring bonded with six phosphate molecules. Therefore, the 6- phytase has the capacity to reduce to an inositol monophosphate molecule and releasing five molecules of inorganic phosphate that could be absorbed by the animals (Kryukov et al., 2021). According to Sands et al. (2001) and Oryschak et al. (2002), every parameter of phosphorus balance was affected by phytase, which is similar to the results obtained in this study (Table 3).

The fecal and excreted phosphorus were decreased according to the increased phytase addition (Table 2), as reported by Almeida and Stein (2012), due to the higher phosphorus digestion. These results show that exogenous phytases can reduce phytate due to its in vivo enzymatic activity since, the more phytase is added into the feed, the less available/total phosphorus is required in the diet, and less phosphorus will be excreted to the environment. In addi-

tion, we found that retained phosphorus linearly decreased with the addition of phytases, but this could be explained mainly with the lower phosphorus intake affecting retention. In many plant- based ingredients, phytic acid is a common compound acting as a way to store phosphorus. Pigs have limited endogenous phytases, having worse phosphorous retention, and excreting more phos- phorus due to their loss of phytic acid (Romano and Kumar, 2018).

The hypothesis of this study was that the addition of phytases, concomitant with the reduction of the available phosphorus con- tent in the form of dicalcium phosphate, could result in a similar environmental impact from pig production while still meeting the minimum available phosphorus requirements. Phytase supple- mentation resulted in the same impacts compared with diets con- taining high available phosphorus content without phytase supplementation.

The final values are expressed per kg of live weight (Table 6). Thus, the environmental impacts are highly dependent on animal performance and feed intake, and performance is linked to feed production and manure (Fig. 3). Efficient pig production could result in a lower ADF and, therefore, a lesser need for feed produc- tion. Manure also has an important share in the environmental impacts (Fig. 3), meaning that efficient production could reduce the 15-30 kg finishing phase, which could lead to fewer days excreting feces and urine.

The environmental impacts of all LCA categories were reduced per kg of diet (Table 5), according to phytase supplementation and dietary dicalcium phosphate reduction. Despite this study not finding any difference in environmental impacts when calcu- lating pig production as a whole, the strategy of using dietary phy- tases could help reduce the environmental impacts of the diets.

However, no effects on GWP, AP, EP, CED and LO were observed while analyzing the production of pigs (Table 6), mainly due to the same performance observed (Table 4). Phytases may provide improved ileal digestibility of amino acids (Kemme et al., 1999), because the phosphorylated phytic acid and its esters (penta-, tetra-, tri-, di- and mono-inositol esters) have amino acid binding capacity (Yu et al., 2012). According to Yu et al. (2012), phytic acid may be hydrolyzed by fungal and E. coli phytases, and the binding capacity was tested in soy protein molecules, including the penta-ester isomers.

The obtained results showed that phytic acid has a strong bind- ing capacity to corn and soybean meal amino acids, with reduced bond strength when the molecule lost its phosphate radicals. The generally accepted concept is that the negatively charged phytate molecules form a binary protein-phytate complex with proteins, carrying a net positive charge at a pH lower than their isoelectric point. At a pH exceeding their isoelectric point, with proteins car- rying a net negative charge, a cationic bridge, usually with Ca2+ links the phytate and protein in salt-like complexes (Selle et al., 2012). However, the exact factors that determine how phytases could actually improve the ileal digestibility of amino acids remains unclear (Selle et al., 2012; Zouaoui et al., 2012). In this sense, the lower N excreted at higher phytase levels studied may be associated with an improved ileal digestibility of amino acids.

According to Sands et al. (2001) and Oryschak et al. (2002), every parameter of phosphorus balance was affected by phytase, which is similar to the results obtained in this study (Table 3).

The fecal and excreted phosphorus were decreased according to the increased phytase addition (Table 2), as reported by Almeida and Stein (2012), due to the higher phosphorus digestion. These results show that exogenous phytases can reduce phytate due to its in vivo enzymatic activity since, the more phytase is added into the feed, the less available/total phosphorus is required in the diet, and less phosphorus will be excreted to the environment. In addi-

tion, we found that retained phosphorus linearly decreased with the addition of phytases, but this could be explained mainly with the lower phosphorus intake affecting retention. In many plant- based ingredients, phytic acid is a common compound acting as a way to store phosphorus. Pigs have limited endogenous phytases, having worse phosphorous retention, and excreting more phos- phorus due to their loss of phytic acid (Romano and Kumar, 2018).

The hypothesis of this study was that the addition of phytases, concomitant with the reduction of the available phosphorus con- tent in the form of dicalcium phosphate, could result in a similar environmental impact from pig production while still meeting the minimum available phosphorus requirements. Phytase supple- mentation resulted in the same impacts compared with diets con- taining high available phosphorus content without phytase supplementation.

Efficient pig production could result in a lower ADF and, therefore, a lesser need for feed produc- tion. Manure also has an important share in the environmental impacts (Fig. 3), meaning that efficient production could reduce the 15-30 kg finishing phase, which could lead to fewer days excreting feces and urine.

The environmental impacts of all LCA categories were reduced per kg of diet (Table 5), according to phytase supplementation and dietary dicalcium phosphate reduction. Despite this study not finding any difference in environmental impacts when calcu- lating pig production as a whole, the strategy of using dietary phy- tases could help reduce the environmental impacts of the diets.

However, no effects on GWP, AP, EP, CED and LO were observed while analyzing the production of pigs (Table 6), mainly due to the same performance observed (Table 4). The results showed that phytases can partially substitute dical- cium phosphate due to its effect in releasing phosphorus molecules bonded into phytic acid. This effect indicates that phytases could reduce the dietary available phosphorus without affecting growth performance. Dietary phytases provided the same ADFI between treatments (Sands et al., 2001) when evaluating 250 and 500 FTU. Phytases may provide improved ileal digestibility of amino acids (Kemme et al., 1999), because the phosphorylated phytic acid and its esters (penta-, tetra-, tri-, di- and mono-inositol esters) have amino acid binding capacity (Yu et al., 2012). According to Yu et al. (2012), phytic acid may be hydrolyzed by fungal and E. coli phytases, and the binding capacity was tested in soy protein molecules, including the penta-ester isomers.

The obtained results showed that phytic acid has a strong bind- ing capacity to corn and soybean meal amino acids, with reduced bond strength when the molecule lost its phosphate radicals. The generally accepted concept is that the negatively charged phytate molecules form a binary protein-phytate complex with proteins, carrying a net positive charge at a pH lower than their isoelectric point. At a pH exceeding their isoelectric point, with proteins car- rying a net negative charge, a cationic bridge, usually with Ca2+ links the phytate and protein in salt-like complexes (Selle et al., 2012). However, the exact factors that determine how phytases could actually improve the ileal digestibility of amino acids remains unclear (Selle et al., 2012; Zouaoui et al., 2012). In this sense, the lower N excreted at higher phytase levels studied may be associated with an improved ileal digestibility of amino acids.

The 6-phytase metabolism pathway acts by reducing the phytic acid (inositol hexaphosphate or IP6), which is composed of an inos- itol ring bonded with six phosphate molecules. Therefore, the 6- phytase has the capacity to reduce to an inositol monophosphate molecule and releasing five molecules of inorganic phosphate that could be absorbed by the animals (Kryukov et al., 2021). According to Sands et al. (2001) and Oryschak et al. (2002), every parameter of phosphorus balance was affected by phytase, which is similar to the results obtained in this study (Table 3).

The fecal and excreted phosphorus were decreased according to the increased phytase addition (Table 2), as reported by Almeida and Stein (2012), due to the higher phosphorus digestion. These results show that exogenous phytases can reduce phytate due to its in vivo enzymatic activity since, the more phytase is added into the feed, the less available/total phosphorus is required in the diet, and less phosphorus will be excreted to the environment. In addi-

tion, we found that retained phosphorus linearly decreased with the addition of phytases, but this could be explained mainly with the lower phosphorus intake affecting retention. In many plant- based ingredients, phytic acid is a common compound acting as a way to store phosphorus. Pigs have limited endogenous phytases, having worse phosphorous retention, and excreting more phos- phorus due to their loss of phytic acid (Romano and Kumar, 2018).

The hypothesis of this study was that the addition of phytases, concomitant with the reduction of the available phosphorus con- tent in the form of dicalcium phosphate, could result in a similar environmental impact from pig production while still meeting the minimum available phosphorus requirements. Phytase supple- mentation resulted in the same impacts compared with diets con- taining high available phosphorus content without phytase supplementation.
